preamble: The transformation of the voiced labio-velar approximant /w/ into the close-mid back rounded vowel /o/ is a natural phonetic shift observed in various languages. This change, often termed vocalization or vowelization, occurs when the consonantal constriction of /w/ weakens, allowing its inherent vocalic qualities—specifically its labial and velar articulation, which contribute to backness and rounding—to become fully realized as a vowel. Such shifts are typically conditioned by specific phonetic environments, such as occurring before consonants or as part of broader lenition processes.

examples.note: Certain stems ending in /w/ change to /o/ before a suffix consisting of a glottal stop.
In Štokavian dialects, as part of a lenition chain where word-final /l/ weakens to /w/ and then vocalizes to /o/.

sources: Greenberg, M. L. (2022). DIJALEKTI, JEZIČNA POVIJEST I TRADICIJA. KU ScholarWorks.
